A solution that fits in your pocket, yet millions of children never receive it

Author

Marie Blanc-Coquand

Published

April 10, 2026

Access to Oral Rehydration Therapy: a global analysis

Oral Rehydration Therapy (ORT) is a precise mix of water, salt and sugar. It costs less than $0.37 and is considered one of the most significant medical innovations of the twentieth century.

And yet, across 116 countries, fewer than 1 in 2 children with diarrhoea receives it.

This report explores where ORT does not reach, how coverage has evolved, and what may predict access to this life-saving treatment.

Where does ORT not reach?

Access to Oral Rehydration Therapy remains highly uneven across the world, revealing strong geographical disparities.

Low coverage is predominantly concentrated in Sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia, where several countries display critically low levels of access. In contrast, higher levels of ORT coverage are observed in Latin America, parts of Asia, and most high-income countries.

Code 
import geopandas as gpd

# Moyenne ORT par pays sur toute la période
ort_country = df.group_by(["country", "alpha_3_code"]).agg(pl.col("ort").mean()).to_pandas()

# Charger les frontières mondiales
world = gpd.read_file("https://naturalearth.s3.amazonaws.com/110m_cultural/ne_110m_admin_0_countries.zip")
wm = world.merge(ort_country, left_on="ADM0_A3", right_on="alpha_3_code", how="left")

(ggplot(wm)
 + geom_map(aes(fill="ort"))
 + scale_fill_gradient(low="#d73027", high="#1a9850", na_value="#eeeeee", name="ORT %")
 + labs(title="Where ORT does not reach", subtitle="Average ORT coverage by country")
 + theme_void()
 + theme(figure_size=(10, 5)))

20 years of slow progress

ORT coverage has stayed almost flat at around 46% for more than 20 years. Even in the best year, 2019 (56.65%), the improvement did not last.

Better ORT → longer lives

There is a positive correlation between ORT coverage and life expectancy at birth. As life expectancy increases, ORT coverage tends to increase too, suggesting a strong link between child healthcare access and broader health outcomes.

ORT is not just a treatment for diarrhoea: it is a marker of how well a health system reaches its most vulnerable.

The countries leading the way

These 15 countries show that high ORT coverage is not only achieved by wealthy nations. Several low and middle income countries rank among the best performers, suggesting that targeted health programmes can make a real difference

Code 
top15 = ort_country.nlargest(15, "ort")

(ggplot(top15, aes(x="reorder(country, ort)", y="ort"))
 + geom_col(fill="#1cabe2")
 + coord_flip()
 + labs(title="Top 15 countries by ORT coverage", x="", y="ORT coverage (%)")
 + theme_minimal()
 + theme(figure_size=(8, 6)))

The takeaway

After 25 years, access to a $0.37 treatment remains deeply unequal. The barriers are not only financial but also structural. Where governments invest in community health, children get treated.

444,000 children under 5 die every year from diarrhoea. — UNICEF, 2024

Data source: UNICEF · Visualisation by Marie Blanc-Coquand · BAA1030 — Dublin City University
